moksha-prapti-stotram: Liberation, not worldly favour, is what this hymn asks for. The मोक्ष प्राप्ति स्तोत्रम् addresses Shiva as the granter of moksha, and across its eleven verses it turns the mind away from the endless round of birth and death. Drawn from the Shaiva Agama tradition, it speaks the language of Advaita: each line works to loosen attachment and awaken Self-knowledge in the reciter. Sannyasis and serious seekers on the path of jnana have long kept it close, partly because it is said to dissolve the fear of death itself along with the grip of worldly bondage. Monday recitation suits its mood, and Maha Shivaratri is the natural night for it. About seven minutes carries you through the full text.
mrityunjaya-kavacham: A kavacham is armour, and the मृत्युञ्जय कवचम् builds it directly from Shiva in his death-conquering form. Eight verses station Mrityunjaya's various aspects across all ten directions, so that no quarter is left unguarded. Devotees turn to it chiefly against apamrityu, the dread of untimely death, but the same recitation is held to draw down longevity, sound health, and freedom from disease. It comes from the Rudrayamala Tantra, a text steeped in protective practice. The shield is felt most strongly when the words are chanted on Maha Shivaratri or a Pradosh evening, and Monday remains the standing day for it. The full recitation runs to roughly eight minutes.
mukunda-mala-stotram: Kulashekhara Alwar, a king who set aside his crown for Krishna, left behind these eleven verses as a garland for Mukunda. The मुकुन्दमाला स्तोत्रम् is unabashed bhakti — it asks for one thing above all, that the lotus feet of the Lord stay fixed in memory, even at the final hour when speech fails. Reading it, the pull of worldly pleasure is meant to thin out; what remains is remembrance. The tradition promises rescue from the ocean of samsara and the blessing of liberation carried across more than one lifetime. Devotees favour it on Wednesday and Thursday, and especially on Janmashtami and Ekadashi. Allow about twelve minutes for an unhurried recitation.
naga-gayatri: For households troubled by snakes — real or astrological — the नाग गायत्री offers a focused remedy. It gathers three Gayatri mantras addressed to the Naga deities, who in tradition belong to Shiva's circle with Vishnu close behind. Eight verses carry the Ashta-naga stuti and the nine sacred Naga names, and within them lies a recognised counter to Kala Sarpa Dosha in the horoscope. Beyond the chart, the chanting is meant to ease the plain fear of serpents and the troubles attributed to them. Nag Panchami is the day it truly belongs to, when serpent worship and appeasement take centre stage. The recitation takes about seven minutes.
naga-stotram: Nine great Nagas — the serpent kings counted among Shiva's attendants, with Vishnu honoured alongside — are summoned by name in this stotra. The नाग स्तोत्रम् draws on the Agni Purana and Garuda Purana, and its Sheshanaga stuti calls down stability and a kind of cosmic steadiness, Shesha being the coil on which the worlds rest. Across nine verses it works as both protection and remedy: shielding against snakebite, easing fear of serpents, and serving as a recognised answer to Kala Sarpa Dosha. There is an old line that reciting the nine Naga names at dawn secures victory everywhere one goes. Nag Panchami sharpens its potency. Set aside ten minutes for the whole.
nageshwar-stotram: Stand before the tenth Jyotirlinga at Nageshwar, or recite these ten verses with attention, and tradition counts the merit as equal. The श्री नागेश्वर स्तोत्रम्, preserved in the Shiva Purana, binds Shiva and Parvati together as the linga's presiding pair. Its reach is wide: protection from snakes and the afflictions of poison, relief associated in tradition with tumours and chronic ailments, and the washing away of sins gathered across lifetimes. What it finally points toward is shiva-sayujya, union with Shiva, and a settled happiness and peace along the way. Monday suits it, as do Maha Shivaratri and Nag Panchami. The recitation comes to about seven minutes.
nama-ramayanam: The whole Ramayana, folded into the names of Rama — that is the working idea behind the नाम रामायणम्. Its 108 lines move through all seven Kandas, from Bala to Uttara, each epithet a doorway into an episode, so that to chant it is held to earn the merit of reading the full epic. Rama stands at the centre, with Sita, Hanuman, and Lakshmana woven through the names. Because it is built from names alone, it can be memorised and carried anywhere, which makes it a favourite for nama-sankirtana. Devotees take it up on Thursday and Saturday, and it rings out at Ram Navami, Hanuman Jayanti, and Dussehra. A complete recitation runs about twelve minutes, offering protection, dharma, and a path toward liberation through Rama's grace.
nandi-stotram: Before you reach Shiva, you greet Nandi — and pleasing the bull who guards the threshold is how the श्री नन्दी स्तोत्रम् opens the way. Drawn from the Shiva Purana, its nine verses praise Shiva's faithful vahana and Parvati's gatekeeper, asking him to clear the obstacles that so often crowd Shiva worship. The reward is access: deep devotion, the felt presence of Shiva, and the yogic knowledge and spiritual wisdom that Nandi, himself a great yogi, is said to bestow. Through his intercession the devotee is finally led toward Shivaloka. Monday and Saturday suit it, with Maha Shivaratri and the Mondays of Shravan especially apt. Six minutes covers the recitation.
narada-stuti: When the sage Narada lifts his voice in the Shrimad Bhagavata Purana, he names Vasudeva as the single goal worth every pursuit — and that is the heart of this stuti. The नारद स्तुति runs nine verses that cut through the confusion sown by the three gunas and bring a plain clarity about why one is alive at all. Krishna stands behind Vishnu here, the two scarcely distinct. The prayer kindles single-pointed devotion to Narayana and, in an age the texts call degrading, is invoked as a guard against the corrosions of Kali Yuga. What it leaves with the reciter is the wisdom Narada himself embodied. Thursday is its day, with Ekadashi and Guru Purnima especially fitting; the reading takes about seven minutes.
narasimha-aarti: At twilight, when day and night belong to neither, Narasimha tore through a pillar — and the नरसिंह आरती is best sung in that same in-between hour. Its six short verses honour the half-man, half-lion avatar of Vishnu, with Lakshmi and the boy Prahlada in attendance. The flame is waved not for gentle blessing but for fierce shelter: against enemies, against injustice, against tyranny of every kind. Sung with feeling, it is held to lift deep-seated fears and put courage in their place, carrying the old assurance that God will appear to defend the devoted. Saturday and Narasimha Jayanti are its occasions, and Sandhya its most charged moment. About five minutes sees it through.
narasimha-ashtakam: Each of these eight verses is, in plain terms, an act of surrender — the reciter hands himself over to Narasimha as a child trusts a father to keep him safe. The नरसिंहाष्टकम्, carried in Vaishnavite tradition with Lakshmi beside the Lord, is aimed squarely at antaka-bhaya, the fear of death that surfaces most in the dark. That is why it is so often taken up in the evening and just before sleep. Beyond protection, it asks for the inner equipment of a devotee: jnana, shraddha, bhakti, and shuddha-sattva — clear knowledge, faith, devotion, pure goodness. The promise is rescue from the ocean of material existence. Recite it on Thursday, Saturday, or at Narasimha Jayanti; about six minutes is enough.
narasimha-dwadash-nama: Twelve names, five short verses, and a great deal of protection packed inside. The नरसिंह द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् from the Narasimha Purana works by simple repetition of Narasimha's twelve sacred names, with Vishnu acknowledged as the source behind the form. Tradition credits it with breaking nightmares, scattering the fear of ghosts, spirits, and other negative entities, and clearing disease from the body. There is a worldly blessing too — wealth, and an abundance of food grains in the home. Brief enough to fit a morning and an evening, it is a natural twice-daily guard. Saturday and Narasimha Jayanti suit it best. The whole thing takes barely three minutes.
narasimha-gayatri: Ugram Veeram Maha-Vishnum — the dhyana shloka most associated with Narasimha sits inside this Gayatri, and it is one of the reasons devotees reach for it when fear has the upper hand. The नरसिंह गायत्री मन्त्र, drawn from the Narasimha Tapaniya Upanishad, invokes the Vajranakha form — claws like thunderbolts — in five verses with the boy-saint Prahlada in view. Of all the Gayatris, this is the one tradition names for destroying fears of every kind and conferring absolute fearlessness. It is also turned against black magic, the evil eye, and hostile forces. The twilight hours and Narasimha Jayanti charge it most strongly, and Saturday is its standing day. Around four minutes carries the recitation.
narasimha-hridayam: Prahlada Maharaja is said to have revealed this one himself — fitting, since the नरसिंह हृदयम् is the very heart of the Lord he loved. Preserved in the Ahirbudhnya Samhita, its ten verses gather Narasimha alongside Lakshmi and Vishnu into a single shield. It is invoked for the removal of fear and, on the worldly side, for perpetual victory; it is also held to drive out disease and evil influence. One of its particular uses is against Bala Graha, the malefic planetary effects that classical texts blame for affliction. As a heart-hymn it is compact and intense rather than sprawling. Saturday and Narasimha Jayanti are its days; about five minutes completes it.
narasimha-kavacham: Prahlada speaks this armour into being — the नरसिंह कवचम् carries the tag Prahlada-uktam in the Brahmanda Purana, the boy-devotee reciting it as protection learned at the source. Across thirteen verses it wraps the reciter, with Lakshmi alongside Narasimha, against enemies and evil forces and against malefic planetary influence. It does more than shield: it is said to burn away sin and grant purity, to bring sons, wealth, and long life, and to hand victory in every undertaking. The texts add, almost plainly, that desires are fulfilled by it without doubt. Thursday, Sunday, and Saturday all suit it, with Narasimha Jayanti the high point. A full, careful recitation runs about twelve minutes.
narasimha-mangalashtakam: Mangala — auspiciousness — gives this ashtakam its name, but the auspiciousness it carries is the kind born of fearlessness. Across eight verses the नरसिंह मङ्गलाष्टकम्, from the Narasimha Purana, invokes Vishnu's lion-man form with Lakshmi and Prahlada near at hand, and devotees hold it to be among the most powerful fear-removing prayers of all. It guards against enemies, evil forces, and black magic, clears obstacles from the path, and is even associated in tradition with the easing of disease. What it finally offers is fearlessness itself and a place in Vishnu Loka. The twilight hours and Narasimha Jayanti lend it special force; Saturday is its weekly day. Five minutes is enough to recite it in full.
narasimha-nakha-stuti: It takes only the nails of the Lord to settle the matter — the नरसिंह नख स्तुति praises Narasimha's claws, the very weapons that ended Hiranyakashipu. Composed by Madhvacharya, the founder of the Dvaita school, these eight verses carry a philosophical edge alongside their devotion, with Vishnu acknowledged as the deity in this terrible form. Devotees recite it for complete victory over enemies and evil forces, and for the lifting of fear, darkness, and ignorance. It stands in the line of supreme protection prayers cherished by devotees like Prahlada, and tradition reaches for it when obstacles loom and courage is needed. Saturday and Narasimha Jayanti are its occasions. About six minutes carries it through.
narasimha-stotram: Narasimha is called bhayam bhayanam — the fear of fears — and these ten verses lean entirely on that name. The नरसिंह स्तोत्रम्, kept alive in Vaishnavite tradition with Lakshmi at the Lord's side, asks for fierce protection from every enemy and evil, and for relief from suffering and sorrow. It dwells on his omnipresence, the sense that the half-lion guards all directions at once, which is why it is so often taken up against black magic and ambient negativity. What it builds in the reciter is courage, valour, and the expectation of victory in whatever is undertaken. Thursday, Sunday, and Saturday suit it, with Narasimha Jayanti the peak day. The recitation comes to about seven minutes.
narayana-ashtakam: Simple on the surface, deep underneath — the नारायणाष्टकम् invokes Narayana's all-pervading presence in eight unhurried verses, drawing on Vaishnavite tradition with Krishna and Lakshmi in its orbit. The closing verses (7 and 8) carry the essence of the Narayana Upanishad, which gives the prayer its weight beyond mere praise. Reciting it is held to dissolve accumulated sin through the sheer power of the Name, and to loosen the fear of samsara. Morning and evening both suit it; it asks little and gives peace, protection, and a lift toward higher ground. Thursday and Sunday are its days, with Ekadashi and Vaikuntha Ekadashi especially apt. About five minutes sees you through it.
narayana-hridayam: This is the Hridayam, the heart — the नारायण हृदयम् claims to hold the essence of Narayana tattva itself, and it backs the claim with structure. Tied to the Atharvana tradition and the sage Bhargava, its nine verses come fully equipped with Nyasa: Karanyasa and Anganyasa that place the names of Narayana on the hands and limbs, with the Vishnu Gayatri embedded in the text. The boldest promise is its phrase Narayana-samo bhavet — the devotee becomes equal to Narayana — and along that road it offers health, wealth, fame, victory, and moksha, with Lakshmi never far. By long custom it is paired with the Lakshmi Hridayam for the complete fruit. Thursday and the Ekadashis suit it; allow about seven minutes.
narayana-jyotirmaya-stotram: Light against darkness — the श्री नारायण ज्योतिर्मय स्तोत्रम् takes Narayana as the Light of all lights and sets him against the inner gloom of ignorance, fear, and low spirits. Its eight verses gather key Upanishadic mantras on the Supreme Light, drawn from the Isha, Katha, and Mundaka and arranged into one devotional thread. Read with attention, it is meant to clear the mind and sharpen spiritual vision, which is why it lends itself so well to meditation on the divine light within. Where there is depression or mental darkness, tradition reaches for it. Sunday and Thursday suit it, and it shines on Diwali, the festival of lamps, as well as Ekadashi and Vaikuntha Ekadashi. About five minutes.
narayana-suktam: From the Taittiriya Aranyaka of the Yajurveda comes the नारायण सूक्तम्, a Vedic hymn that places the Supreme Being not in some far heaven but within the cavity of the heart. Its fourteen verses awaken that recognition slowly, purifying mind and consciousness as they go. The tradition speaks of deep inner peace and a quiet spiritual illumination that settles over the reciter. It scatters ignorance and confers divine knowledge, and points finally toward liberation and Self-realisation, with all-round protection and prosperity along the way. Few hymns suit daily meditation as well. Thursday and Sunday are its standing days, with Ekadashi especially auspicious. A measured recitation takes about ten minutes.
narayana-upanishad: Short in extent, vast in claim — the नारायण उपनिषद् from the Atharva Veda runs to just three sections, yet it is reckoned among the most important Vedic Upanishads devoted to Narayana. What it reveals is direct: Narayana as the ultimate reality, the ground of all that is. To chant it is held to lift ignorance and open the way to liberation, and to confer the highest spiritual knowledge and Self-realisation. The deeper gift is connection — a settling of the mind into peace and contact with the Absolute. Thursday and Monday suit it, and it is especially apt on Ekadashi and Guru Purnima. Recited with the care its content deserves, it occupies about ten minutes.
narmada-ashtakam: Adi Shankaracharya composed these eight verses to the Narmada in a metre that moves like the river itself. There is an old saying the नर्मदाष्टकम् takes to heart — that even the stones lifted from her bed become Shivalingas, which is why the Narmada is honoured, for the purpose of self-formed linga worship, above the Ganga. Across its verses the river-goddess is invoked to wash away sin, disease, and sorrow when the hymn is recited thrice daily, and to lift the fear of death and Yama's messengers. What it kindles is a sage's courage and the hope of liberation. Monday suits it, and Narmada Jayanti is its festival. About seven minutes carries the recitation.
narmada-stotram: Compact and devotional, the नर्मदा स्तोत्रम् gathers the Narmada in all her forms into five short verses. Shiva is honoured close by, fitting, given how intimately the river and the Lord of the Linga are bound in the Puranic tradition that preserves this hymn. It is recited chiefly to wash away sin and gather spiritual merit, and it asks the goddess for protection and an air of auspiciousness over the household. Brief enough for daily worship, it also rises to the river's own festivals. Maha Shivaratri suits it well. Monday is its weekly day. The full recitation takes only about three minutes, which makes it easy to keep as a steady habit.
narsimha-chalisa: Forty chaupais give the form its name — chalis is forty — and with the framing dohas the नृसिंह चालीसा runs to forty-two verse-units in all. In the familiar Chalisa mould, it praises Narasimha, Vishnu's most protective avatar, and it is sung above all for shelter from enemies and evil forces. Where there are deep-seated fears and phobias, it is reached for; where powerful adversaries throw up obstacles, it is held to clear them. What it builds in the reciter is courage and a steadier nerve in hard times. Saturday and Sunday are its days, and Narasimha Jayanti its festival. A full recitation runs about fifteen minutes.
nasadiya-suktam: Then there was neither being nor non-being — so opens the नासदीय सूक्तम्, the most celebrated philosophical hymn of the Rigveda, set down at 10.129. In seven verses it asks where creation came from, and dares to wonder whether even the gods, who came after, can know; perhaps the One who oversees it all from the highest heaven knows, or perhaps not even He. It is less an act of worship than an act of inquiry, opening the mind to the mystery that stands before all creation and inviting genuine self-questioning. Scholars across the world count it among humanity's earliest cosmological texts. About five minutes to read, and far longer to sit with.
naukari-prapti-stotram: Job-seekers know the weight of an interview the morning of — and the नौकरी प्राप्ति स्तोत्रम् is built for exactly that pressure. Rooted in Niti Shastra, it does not lean on a single deity but gathers several: Vishnu at the centre, with Ganesha, Saraswati, Hanuman, and Shani all called upon for employment and advancement. Crucially, it invokes the planetary lords Shani, Budh, and Guru, whose alignment tradition ties to career fortune. Its eight verses are aimed at clearing the obstacles that stall a job hunt and at carrying the reciter through interviews and competitive exams. Custom says eleven days of steady recitation brings good news. Wednesday, Thursday, and Saturday suit it; barely four minutes to recite.
navadurga-gayatri: One Gayatri for each night of Navratri — the नवदुर्गा गायत्री sets out nine mantras, one apiece for the nine forms of Durga from Shailaputri to Siddhidatri, with Parvati behind them all. Drawn from the Devi Purana, it lets a devotee match the day's worship to the day's form, so the vrata unfolds in proper order. Recited systematically through the nine nights, it is held to confer the siddhis and the full nine-fold grace of the Navadurga. The same Gayatri power is invoked against disease, fear, and enemies, and for prosperity, health, and steady spiritual progress. Tuesday and Friday suit it outside the festival, and Navratri is its true season. About seven minutes for the set.
navarna-mantra-vidhana: Aim, Hreem, Kleem — three beejas, nine syllables, and the seed of the entire Durga Saptashati. The नवार्ण मन्त्र विधान lays out the Navarna mantra as the Mula Mantra of the Saptashati, the one mantra without which no recitation of that text is complete. Drawn from the Saptashati in the Markandeya Purana, it summons the triple Goddess at once — Mahakali, Mahalakshmi, Mahasaraswati — with each beeja firing Power, Desire, and Knowledge in turn. This is a full vidhana, not a bare mantra: Viniyoga, Nyasa, Dhyana, and the Japa Vidhi are all set down. For Navratri, tradition prescribes 972 repetitions a day for the highest fruit. Tuesday and Friday suit it, and Durga Puja and Chaitra Navratri are its festivals; about eight minutes to read the procedure through.
